gold_final_answer:
All real-coefficient polynomials P are:
- If b = 0: P(x) = c for any real constant c.
- If k = b/a is a natural number: P(x) = c · x(x − a)(x − 2a) ··· (x − (k − 1)a) for any real constant c.
- If k = b/a is not a natural number: P(x) ≡ 0.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Polynomials > Polynomial operations
prompt:
Let $a, b$ be given two real numbers with $a \neq 0$. Find all polynomials $P$ with real coefficients such that
$$
x P(x-a) = (x-b) P(x) \text{ for all } x \in \mathbb{R}.
$$
response_text:
Let \(P\) be a real polynomial satisfying
\[
xP(x-a)=(x-b)P(x)\qquad \forall x\in\mathbb R,
\]
with \(a\neq 0\).

We will determine all possibilities.

## 1) First observations
If \(P\equiv 0\), the identity is true. So assume \(P\not\equiv 0\), and let \(\deg P=n\) with leading coefficient \(c\neq 0\).

Then
- left side: \(xP(x-a)\) has leading term \(x\cdot c x^n = c x^{n+1}\),
- right side: \((x-b)P(x)\) has leading term \(x\cdot c x^n = c x^{n+1}\),

so degrees match automatically.

Now compare the coefficients of \(x^n\).  
Write
\[
P(x)=cx^n+\text{(lower terms)}.
\]
Then
\[
P(x-a)=c(x-a)^n+\cdots = c\left(x^n-na\,x^{n-1}+\cdots\right)+\cdots
\]
so
\[
xP(x-a)=cx^{n+1}-cna\,x^n+\cdots.
\]
Also,
\[
(x-b)P(x)=cx^{n+1}+(d-bc)x^n+\cdots
\]
where \(d\) is the coefficient of \(x^{n-1}\) in \(P\). Equating the \(x^n\)-coefficients alone is not enough to finish directly, so we use roots.

## 2) Root propagation
If \(P(r)=0\), then from the identity at \(x=r\),
\[
rP(r-a)=(r-b)P(r)=0.
\]
Hence if \(r\neq 0\), then \(P(r-a)=0\). So any nonzero root generates a root shifted by \(-a\), and then again, etc. This would produce infinitely many roots unless the process stops, impossible for a nonzero polynomial.

Therefore, the only possible root of \(P\) is \(0\). Hence
\[
P(x)=cx^n
\]
for some \(c\neq 0\), \(n\ge 0\).

## 3) Substitute \(P(x)=cx^n\)
Then
\[
x\cdot c(x-a)^n=(x-b)\cdot cx^n.
\]
Cancel \(c\):
\[
x(x-a)^n=(x-b)x^n.
\]

If \(n=0\), this becomes
\[
x=x-b \quad\forall x,
\]
so \(b=0\), and every constant polynomial works.

Now assume \(n\ge 1\). Compare the constant term: the left side has constant term \(0\), while the right side has constant term \(0\) too, so no issue. Instead evaluate at \(x=0\): both sides are \(0\). Better, compare roots:
- The right side has roots \(x=0\) (with multiplicity \(n\)) and \(x=b\).
- The left side has roots \(x=0\) and \(x=a\) (with multiplicity \(n\) at \(x=a\) unless \(a=0\), which is excluded).

For the identities to hold as polynomials, the sets of roots with multiplicities must match. This forces \(n=1\) and \(b=a\): indeed
\[
x(x-a)= (x-a)x
\]
works exactly when \(b=a\).

So for \(n\ge 1\), the only solution is \(n=1\), \(b=a\), and \(P(x)=cx\).

## 4) Check the solutions
- If \(b=0\), every constant polynomial \(P(x)=c\) works:
  \[
  x c=(x-0)c.
  \]
- If \(b=a\), every polynomial of the form \(P(x)=cx\) works:
  \[
  x\cdot c(x-a)=(x-a)\cdot cx.
  \]
- The zero polynomial always works.

No other solutions exist.

---

Final answer: The solutions are:
- \(P(x)\equiv 0\) for all \(a,b\);
- if \(b=0\), any constant polynomial \(P(x)=c\);
- if \(b=a\), any linear polynomial of the form \(P(x)=cx\).

Equivalently, the nonzero solutions are exactly:
\[
\boxed{\begin{cases}
P(x)=c & \text{if } b=0,\\[2mm]
P(x)=cx & \text{if } b=a,
\end{cases}
\quad c\in\mathbb R,}
\]
with the zero polynomial always included.
